posts.name: Bertrand Leulliette
posts.cooked:
My fellow Berserkers,

I have just returned from almost a month in Burgundy and I would like to share the following info for those of you ITB or not.

As you have seen the price of the wine have gone up considerably since the 2017 vintage that was and will probably be the last affordable vintage as most of the 2018s were impacted by the Tariffs and the 2019s were so acclaimed that I doubt there are many more around to be had.

On top of this, you all know how difficult transportation has been to bring wine into the country with deliveries taking closer to 90 to 120 days versus 30 a few years back and cost of freight having doubled. The only bit of good news is that the EUR/USD rate has been somewhat stable around 1.1 versus closer to 1.2 a year ago.

On the producer side, raw material is also a nightmare with cost of glass up 30% and that does not actually mean that you get delivered on time even if you pay more, lots of small Domaines have wine stabilized in tanks and ready for bottling but no bottles in sight. On the cork front, I am assuming we all remember those fires in Portugal a few years back, well this has also resulted in a 40% price increase in the cost of corks and since the circle would not be closed without the printers struggling to keep up with raw material for printing, the metal that is used for the foil is also problematic and I have producers offering to ship without foil.

This is a dark picture that gets a bit darker as the frost from the first weekend of April although it did not truly affect the Cote d’Or wines, definitely affected some areas of Chablis pretty bad and some producers have told me that it is “as bad or worst than 2021 in some parts”. The frost of the second weekend (last weekend) although not as impressive as far as the low temperatures did more damage across the Cote d’Or as it had rained all week leading to the frost and the vines got frosty with almost 100% humidity in many parts, it will take a few days still to see that actual impact but I don’t see a “break” headed our way for a large 2022 crop, unless there are no more hiccups until harvest.

In conclusion, I am suggesting that if you are offered 2018 and 2019, pull the trigger as the 2020s are much much more expensive and honestly equally good but maybe less seductive and more ups and downs for reds) the whites 2020 are the true Unicorn to get as there will be so little 2021 that this will definitely keep a few lovers’ mouths dry for Burgundy.

Cote Chalonnaise produced several great vintages in a row since 2016 and they are with appellations like Santenay, Saint-Romain, Auxey-Duresses or Pernand amazing wines to be had for both reds and whites.

The Maconnais 2020s are stunning… and the 1er Cru in Pouilly-Fuisse are truly stunning and justify the move to the higher level of appellation that I tasted.

Let’s talk a little bit about 2021: I sampled a few hundred wines from the vintage and the whites will definitely have this high acidity profile that many are looking for with lower alcohol (around 12 for most of the wines I tasted) with great potential for age-ability in my opinion. The reds are going to be leaner with light colors and also fairly high acids but I feel like (from what I tasted) they will turn out to be enjoyable wines, although clearly dramatically different from most vintages since 2015. THey will clearly not seem like bargains but I fear that the ceiling will not be broken any time soon and they will still sell as the volumes are truly small.

Hope you enjoy the note…

Your Burgundy Servant,

Bertrand

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Hmmmm. We shall see I suppose, but I wouldn’t think of buying ‘18 because of anything I’ve tasted for 2020. I think the 2020 reds are going to be pretty special. Between the amazing freshness of the fruit and the quality of tannins - thick but not grainy or coarse - I think it’s likely to be the best vintage for me in a very long time. The price is, unfortunately, the price. All asset prices seem to be up.
Alex

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: This is a strange time in Burgundy. The wines have turned into a true commodity. Low production. Huge demand. For the true lover of Burgundy, it is so hard to get the wines. I hope that we will see some areas that are relative bargains. I am resigned to the fact that it will be difficult. Thanks for this very sobering but honest report. 2021 from my small world seems to be almost a lost vintage. No quantity and variable quality. FWIW.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
I’m getting daily emails with offers for 2020 whites trying to convince me to buy their overpriced offerings because 2020 is slim and the 2021 and maybe 2022 are going to be non existent so I better stock up now!

Luckily I have amassed more white Burg in the past few years than I can drink in a lifetime at the rate I’m going so I’ll just hold out to 2023 or 2030 or whatever.

posts.name: Daniel. W i l l i g e r
posts.cooked: “ if you are offered 2108 and 2019 pull the trigger.” No and yes. If you like overly extracted Burgundy the 2018 is fruity delicious and simple and not complex. The 2019 is much better. This is just a broad generalization, but to my palate just pass on 2018.

posts.cooked: Also pricing on 2020 depends. Were there massive increases for some producers? Sure. For smaller / lesser known / less established producers, I’ve seen prices either only go up slightly or even hold.

posts.name: Mike Reff
posts.cooked:
Question for the masses. Purchase past vintages or wait until the low yield 2021’s come out and pounce on those?

Or if funds are available do both?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
Mike_Reff:

if funds are available do both?

I have tasted none of the aforementioned vintages but, based on past experience, if one loves Burgundy: buy both. The vintages develop differently and first taste may not be how they taste years hence. Also it’s wonderful to look at vintage variation and not just top years. Look at reds from 2003–hot and unbalanced, overroasted at first, many now quite delicious. Look at 92 and 93 whites, the former a hot “California” vintage, the next a shrill acidic one—both lovely in their own ways now. Buy both.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Old Vine Notes

Old Vine Notes

Barzelay who worked with Burghound makes an excellent yearly assessment of the wines of top producers with a bit of revisiting of the previous year. One may read his reports before drawing definitive decisions… especially given prices…

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: I was going to post tis myself. I loved his commentary on the new hot producers and their prices.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Mike_Reff:

Question for the masses. Purchase past vintages or wait until the low yield 2021’s come out and pounce on those?

Or if funds are available do both?

I think it depends on a lot of factors. Do you like lighter styles of Burgundy or do you like riper Burgundy? Do you want vintages to drink sooner? Do you expect to get offered allocation pricing? Are you trying to get allocations? It really is a combination of factors, and depends what producers you’re looking at.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Thank you for sharing the link to Barzelay’s site, don’t think I’ve seen it before. Very fair and honest reporting, though very much focused on the untouchable (for me) ultra-high end of the market.

What really stunned me was this about Lafarge’s 2021s: “There was not much to taste this year, as all the Volnay premier crus were combined into a single cuvée.” Wow. Has this ever happened before?

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
I note that Barzelay list of producers changes a bit from year to year. There may be several explanations:

  • He could not include the (top) domaine visit in his schedule (these things happen including cancelled appointments)
  • The wines of the producer did not perform to its hypercultish status and, rather than report a disappointing performance, he may want to avoid bringing prejudice and not report as, especially for very young wine tastings, there is the risk that the wines will end up OK or even excellent.
  • There are up and coming producers he may want to include and there is not room for everybody so he may have selected…

Given his general judgment on the vintage “… I am reminded of Winston Churchill’s description of his political rival Clement Attlee as “a modest man with much to be modest about.” With slight adaptation, this might well serve as an epigraph for the 2021 vintage. That said, conditions were far from disastrous, and the best producers made wines that are very good and even, in a few cases, transcend the vintage…”, it would be prudent to taste before committing to large expenses (?)

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: He tends to visit a slightly different group of producers every year, with some changes. He’s not a professional or in the industry - just an individual that is passionate about burgundy. I think tasting is nice in theory, but is a little tricky with burgundy if you are in the US as there isn’t much offered. I’m off to London next week for en primeur week, which this year is as allocated as the wines. So on many of these you just have to take a leap of faith or pass completely, although I think Alan Weinberg’s advice up the thread about the joy of vintage variation is really the right way to go.

posts.name: Bertrand Leulliette
posts.cooked: I spent two weeks tasting nothing but 2021s in December and they will pleasantly surprise everyone as they essentially were deemed to be a much lesser vintage. I find them pure and precise and reminiscent of what put the region on the map.

Best

Antoine

“I began writing reports on my annual barrel tastings several years ago, in response to the many questions I got from friends each year on my return, asking what I thought about the vintage and which producers had done well. I am not in the wine trade nor do I have any ambitions (or illusions) about being a journalist.”

Old Vine Notes – 8 Sep 10

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Just got my 2021 Bouchard EP offer. Prices up substantially for the third year in a row. Monty up 45%. Genevrieres up 50%. Beze/BM up 35%+.

Obviously this was driven by extremely low yields, but after 30% bump for 2019 and 40% bump for 2020, we’ve now seen prices double across the board from 2018 to 2021. Hard to find value anymore and very hard to pull the trigger on 2021 when I haven’t read from anyone that tasted in barrel since they didn’t offer (m)any visits.

Probably just going to roll the dice on a few 3 packs and leave it there.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I would think his reference to a specific plot in Vosne would be directed to Bizot Jachées.

I tend to think one can “believe in terroir” without being so keen an adherent to the hierarchy of terroirs as enshrined in the existing classification.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
Yes Bizot wha what I was thinking. But I felt he was referring to Arnoux. A few years ago I was buying the bourgogne rouge for $20 a bottle and selling for $60 at my restaurant. Now what does it go for ? The clos Vougeot sells for close to $3K.

Also I should point out that he did not malign the quality of these producers. Just that the prices have skyrocketed unrelated to the rise in Burgundy prices in general. Also that their are producers that produce higher quality wine that sell for much more rational prices.

I personally agree. Obviously issues of quality and price are relative but it was nice to see some sane and intelligent commentary. The Burgundy market in particular has become a haven for irrational hyperbole that benefits primarily the retailers. Obviously this is all subjective but if anyone feels that Arnoux RSV is worth more than DRC than rock on. I am voting with my pocketbook.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
I attended Howard Ripley’s en primeur tasting in London today. I didn’t attempt to take any formal notes but tried to get general impressions of the producers and the vintage. In many cases the wines were shown besides their counterpart from ‘20, ‘19 or ‘17, which made for a lot of fun. My impressions of the ‘21 vintage are that it is consistent with what has been advertised; that is, pretty good if you are a fan of a lighter-style burgundy with moderate alcohol and decent structure. The highlights for me were Rene Bouvier’s Gevrey Racine du Temps, which feels like a wine that should get more attention than it does (and the marsannay clos du roy was very nice as well), the Hudelot Baillet charmes, and the Drouhin clos des mouches rouge, which showed really well. The biggest surprise for me were the Camille Giroud wines, with which I’m not at all familiar and really enjoyed (including earlier vintages that were being shown). I will explore deeper. I like the Mugneret-Gibourg vosne but thought the sample of the chambolle feuselottes was likely not representative of what the final wine will be. There were side-by-side bonnes mares from Hudelot-Baillet and Jerome Castagnier, with the former being surprisingly open and the latter being unsurprisingly shut. What does one extrapolate from baby bonnes mares being shut down or open at this time? Not much I suppose but it sure was fun to try them. I thought Barraud showed well (no surprise there) and that the ‘21 vintage was favorable to the style at Domaine de la Grosse Pierre. There was a good bit of talk of prices and quantities.

At a dinner the night before with generous friends we started off with the ‘97 Grunhaus auslese from a numbered fuder (can’t remember if it was 87? 89?) that was almost dry and almost perfect. What a showing. Cedric Bouchard’s ‘06 creux d’enfer was in great shape - wonderful, fruity rose and a rare treat. ‘15 Keller Pettenthal GG was my bottle and it was good but perhaps a bit out of place. ‘88 Rousseau Clos St. Jacques just kept getting better and better throughout the night and was probably WOTN by the end of the evening. ‘99 DRC grand echezeaux was among the top DRC I’ve tried. In a perfect spot right now. Keller 2010 absterde auslese auction was a revelation for me. His wines have gotten leaner over the years (both GG and pradikat) and I wonder whether the more recent auslese are still in the same baroque style. Incredibly big and open but it didn’t lose any of its precision throughout the night. I just loved it.

Alex

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
Neal Martin published a short screed today titled Burgundy 2021: The Market

Nothing particularly new. And especially well-trodden material for WBers. It’s a rant, but a deserved one.

Money quote for me:

“To put it in simple terms, the fun is sucked out of drinking wines that, in the past, have left me and so many others spellbound.”

posts.name: Brady Daniels
posts.cooked:
I listened to Jasper Morris’s podcast, “Jasper Morris Inside Burgundy” and he seemed quite realistic about the wines, no hype machine there.

He concluded by agreeing it was somewhat a return to classic vintage style, but explained that these sorts of classic vintages used to be a lot cheaper, and generally not subject to price increases (especially after 2020 increases).

He also made clear no domaine equated 2021 with 2010.

Jasper seems to fear that Burgundy might have gone a bridge too far on pricing, and suggests consumers react accordingly. All in a very understated, gentlemanly, British way, of course.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng

Bertrand_Leulliette:

I would probably go with 2016.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked: Agree, most of the 18’ white Burgs I’ve had were kind of over-the-top big or lacking balance. Not bad, but not stellar. YMMV

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked: Every '18 I’ve had (I’ve only had 5-6) has been super ripe and kinda flabby.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
The problem with '18 WB is many producers made bad wine. But the top producers produced nicely balanced specimens that have enough acidity to balance the ripeness. They will be earlier drinkers, but buy confidently from producers like Leflaive, Lafon, Ramonet, Lamy-Pillot, Moreau.

Those people that aren’t happy unless the acidity peels the enamel off your teeth won’t be happy with anything from 2018 or most vintages for that matter. But if you like balanced wines that drink well early while you’re waiting on your 14s and 17s to come around, there’s good value out there.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: Worth noting that flabby and opulent white burgundy vintages sometimes turn out wines that can be long-lived and incredible. See for example @JasperMorris on the 1947 vintage.

posts.name: Mike Reff
posts.cooked: Two four packs of Girard Les Serpentines and Les Lavieres - 2021 not too shabby of a price.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
Agree.
18’ Niellon Chassagne Champs-Gain (always a favorite), 18’ H. Boillot Perrieres and 18’ Fichet Meursault / Meursault 1er Tessons stood out positively for me. Many others, not so much.

I think 2018 whites will get better from here. Alcohol levels are not especially high, because of the exceptionally high yields which I think have saved the vintage from the excess summer sunshine. There is a lack of grip to some of them, perhaps more a question of dilution than flab, and I am finding that they are beginning to develop more personality. May not be a top contender but I have some optimism.

On pricing, I think it is important to distinguish between the producer and the market place being responsible for some of the very high prices and it is not always easy to work out. I suspect that in the case of Lamy Caillat, the market is being distorted after the wines are offered by the producer, though I do not know the detail. Some charge very high prices ex domaine. Bad will starts to come into play if consumers think they are being taken advantage of by the producers, rather than accepting that it is genuinely the market itself which has pushed the prices.

Current excesses in the market are making me reflect on how to operate as a wine critic, In an ideal world a positive review would enable those who enjoy that style of the wine to be able to buy and enjoy, without any massive shifting of price. One might thoroughly recommend a wine at $50 which would make no sense at $100 let alone $500+. I try to approach it in what Brady, up-thread, kindly described as an understated way, Finding the right wine at the right price is the key, rather than unveiling the next new superstar.
